item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: To be fair, each Addendum tells a story of its own.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know this is pretty insignificant to the SCP in its entirety, but I think the descriptions of the victims would overall sound clinically better if the victims were referred to by their last name

Great SCP though!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Too random for me. There is no story behind it (that I can see, that is). I might have liked that ten years ago, but I have grown out of this stuff by now. I find nothing particularly wrong with it, it is just not my cup of tea (any more?). I cannot echo the above statement that the examples do not add anything new: They show how SCP-4676 affects people in decreasing remoteness, in other words that places which might have been deemed safe from it in fact aren't.

Conclusion: No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Maybe they sent a lawyer to talk to that decapitated french head, and this was one of the things he let slip?

For real though, they have a TON of ways of getting impossible-to-get information. Most of which are top-secret enough that they wouldn't necessarily be mentioned in a containment procedures document.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We don't need to invent "thought-activated SOS" magic nonsense for this. The autonomous nervous system is unaffected, and the victim is capable of noticing their predicament and thinking about it. Therefore SCP-4676 can be detected by implants checking for plain old adrenaline and cortisol.

Of course, you'll get a lot of false positives from Foundation employees being attacked by monsters, suffering containment breaches, being kidnapped by various GOIs… basically anything that would make an employee panic. But I honestly see that as a feature, not a bug.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't know why I'm only seeing your comment just now. Sorry.

One of the reviewers of the draft said the same thing. He/she suggested that I should've added more like an interview but since I'm really not good at writing one, I decided not to. It's really clear now that the reason why the response to this is lukewarm is due to it not having "more".

I'll try my best in adding more content to this in the future. Thanks for checking it out though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really wish there was more to this article. It's one of those rare concepts that has depth while being so simple, and makes itself so much room for being expanded on. Yet the article in its current state sort of glosses over the basics, each example adding very little that hasn't already been covered. I'd like to see, for example, what the Foundation is doing to try to contain it - stating that containment is impossible is a cop-out. You've set out the rules of the SCP but you haven't tried to break them, and that's where the most fun is.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
How can the Foundation be so sure that this is anomalous? No really, hear me out.
For how few "cases" there are, why couldn't this just be random strokes or seizures?

Some of the other details aside, for me, the Foundation is smart enough to know that it wasn't some random strokes or seizures because stuff like that is easily or at least can be detected in autopsies.

I could add a footnote explicitly saying that the victims had no prior illness/abnormalities that may have caused the paralysis nor any signs of seizures or strokes.
open block
close block

Nowhere in this article have I read anything in particular that indicates anomalous activity.

What happened to Harolds was quite anomalous. Even if he was slowly devoured by the insects, he only move after making physical contact with another human. That's weird.

And also these,

SCP-4676 only affects individuals who are alone at the time of onset, and preferentially targets those at least 55 meters from any other human being or human habitation1.

Most of the victims are found at a very specific location/distance and,

SCP-4676 events have no known maximum duration, ending only upon nullification via physical contact with another human being or the death of the victim.

Although not explicitly stated, the Foundation has witnessed these people only starting to move after making physical contact with another human.

You know what they say about hearing hoofbeats and leaping straight to thinking about zebras

Not really. When I hear hoofbeats, I just think "Hmm, could it be a cow or a horse, better check it out". Sometimes, you can recognize who the hoofbeats belong to because horses are rhythmic and cows are usually slow and heavy.

Suspension of disbelief shattered by Occam's razor.

Since we have inherently different views on things, I can't do anything about this.

Thanks for reading it though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So….

How can the Foundation be so sure that this is anomalous? No really, hear me out.
For how few "cases" there are, why couldn't this just be random strokes or seizures? Perfectly vanilla. Nowhere in this article have I read anything in particular that indicates anomalous activity.

You know what they say about hearing hoofbeats and leaping straight to thinking about zebras…

Suspension of disbelief shattered by Occam's razor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Plays off of human fears well, and the transition to social isolation makes for an interesting ending. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nicely understated body horror. Nothing splashy or gory, but a monstrous way to die.

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Special thanks to OriCat for checking out the first version of this article and for his/her very thorough line-by-line critique that basically turned the grammar and clinical tone of this from "okayish" to "now that's clinical tone".

And yet again, another huge thanks to the critters from the Ƹ̵̡Ӝ̵̨̄Ʒ Butterfly Squad Ƹ̵̡Ӝ̵̨̄Ʒ - Stallmantic, Leveritas, and RockTeethMothEyes, for their always invaluable feedbacks. A big thanks to caspian2 for giving his/her equally invaluable feedback and critique via pm.

Second Image - here
